content_en:
Nihayat al-Muhtaj covers the full range of Shafi'i jurisprudence with a depth and precision that has made it one of the definitive references of the school. In the chapters on ritual purity and prayer, ar-Ramli addresses the standard topics — the categories of water, the conditions for wudu and ghusl, the conditions and pillars of prayer — with particular attention to the edge cases and unusual scenarios that generate disagreement among scholars. His treatment of the prayer of the sick and the rules for combining and shortening prayers while traveling is especially thorough.

In commercial law, the Nihaya is notable for its treatment of complex financial transactions. The chapters on salam (forward sale), sarf (currency exchange), and shirkah (partnership) engage with the commercial realities of the Ottoman economic environment. Ar-Ramli addresses the rules for guaranty (kafala) and agency (wakala) with attention to practical applications, and the chapter on waqf engages with the elaborate endowment structures characteristic of Ottoman urban life, including family endowments, mosque endowments, and endowments for schools.

The chapters on marriage and divorce contain some of the Nihaya's most carefully argued legal discussions. Ar-Ramli treats contested questions — such as the conditions for the validity of a marriage contracted without a guardian, the rights of a woman to initiate divorce proceedings, and the rules for nursing and custody — with careful attention to both the primary sources and the positions of earlier Shafi'i authorities. These discussions reveal a scholar aware that legal rules affect real families and that precision in formulation is not merely an academic virtue.

One area where the Nihaya makes a distinctive contribution is the law of endowments and charitable trusts. Egypt in the Ottoman period was characterized by an elaborate waqf economy, and ar-Ramli's detailed treatment of this topic reflects both his command of the Shafi'i tradition and his practical engagement with the legal problems of his environment.
